concepts_addressed: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions

frameworks.name: Two-Vector Learning Framework
frameworks.definition: Intelligence can be improved on two independent axes: reducing the number of iterations required to change behavior, OR compressing the time between iterations so the total elapsed time to behavior change is lower.

frameworks.name: Skill as Chained Behaviors
frameworks.definition: A skill is a sequence of individually learned same-condition/new-behavior pairs chained together; each link in the chain is a discrete learning event.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi defines learning as same condition, new behavior: a person has learned only when they produce a different behavior in the exact same triggering situation. If the condition recurs and the behavior does not change, no learning has occurred.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi defines intelligence as the speed of learning, measured as a rate: specifically, the number of iterations (repeated exposures to the same condition) required before behavior changes. Fewer iterations to change behavior = higher intelligence; more iterations required = lower intelligence.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Intelligence is not a fixed capacity but a rate; the rate is observable via iteration count, which makes it a measurable and improvable variable rather than a fixed trait.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a person who requires more iterations to learn (lower iteration efficiency) can still outperform a faster learner in net elapsed time by compressing the gap between iterations. On a timeline basis, ten iterations completed in a single day beats three iterations spread over three weeks, making the high-volume learner functionally smarter by the metric that matters: time to behavior change.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: change how quickly you do them
conditions: Learner has the ability to compress iteration gaps (e.g., schedule intensive practice blocks)
claims.mechanism: Elapsed time to behavior change is the product of (iterations required) x (time between iterations); a learner who cannot reduce iteration count can still reduce the product by shrinking inter-iteration time.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the domain requires real-world elapsed time between iterations (e.g., running a quarterly business cycle)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that a skill is a chain of adapted behaviors: it is built by sequentially mastering multiple same-condition/new-behavior learning events. The chain of learned behaviors, taken together, constitutes the skill.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that people who feel they have below-average intelligence relative to their peers can compensate by working more hours per day, thereby increasing iteration velocity. While peers delay iterations across weeks, a high-volume worker executes multiple iterations per day, achieving faster behavior change on a calendar basis.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: lock in and do more iterations
claims.topic: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions
conditions: Learner perceives themselves as having lower natural learning speed than peers
Domain allows for rapid iteration (e.g., academic study, skill practice)
claims.mechanism: Higher daily work volume compresses inter-iteration gaps, which multiplies effective learning rate and can overcome a lower per-iteration efficiency advantage held by a faster-learning peer.
bounded_by: Does not apply to domains where real-world time (e.g., market cycles, physical recovery) constrains iteration frequency

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that people who notice others advancing faster than them and resent it are receiving a signal about their own relative intelligence, which he reframes as good news: because intelligence is a rate (not a fixed trait), the gap is controllable through iteration velocity rather than innate capacity.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Reframing intelligence as a rate converts a fixed-trait belief (which blocks action) into a process variable (which invites action).

examples.text: Alex Hormozi illustrates the definition of learning with a door-answering exercise: a trainer tells someone to answer the door with 'welcome to Starbucks' instead of their habitual greeting. On the next knock, if the person says 'welcome to Starbucks,' learning has occurred (same condition: knock-knock; new behavior: Starbucks greeting). If they revert to the old greeting, no learning occurred. The number of knock-knock trials required before the person consistently says 'welcome to Starbucks' measures how intelligent they are relative to the task.

Alex Hormozi describes his time at Vanderbilt University (ranked approximately top 5-7 in the US at the time of the video), where he perceived himself as having below-average intelligence relative to his classmates. His solution was to work from 9 AM to 9 PM every day exclusively in the library, pausing only for class, the cafeteria, and the gym. By running more iterations per day than his peers, he was able to advance faster than people he believed had superior intellectual capacity: his 10 compressed iterations outpaced their 3 slower iterations on a calendar basis.
examples.topic: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions

practices.text: When you need to learn a new skill faster than peers, identify how many iterations you require and schedule them as densely as possible (e.g., all in one day) rather than spacing them out over weeks.

If you feel outpaced by smarter peers, reframe the gap as an iteration-velocity problem, not a fixed-capacity problem, and respond by increasing daily practice volume rather than accepting the deficit as permanent.
practices.topic: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions
